author: @-robo-
text: You might consider using some working ink to paint a heater inside. That way you can also use it for annealing FDM 3D prints as well (while promoting your ink at the same time.)  Also, instead of simple switches, use a Raspberry Pi (or equiv) to allow the user to select how long the UV exposure should run. So many additional options when you add a Pi. So many videos you can make. Also, you might want to consider using UV  epoxy to make battery and capacitor cases. You can paint the epoxy by hand, or 3D print something more precise. Also, I wonder if there would be a way to use UV epoxy as a component of the electrolyte  in a "solid state" battery. Also.. just kidding, I have lots of ideas but this is all I will post for now.

author: @dans-designs
text:
Nice job! I love Resin printing!!
My setup is a little less sophisticated! I use an old washing tablet box (bright white plastic inside) with the lid taken off and a cheap UV floodlight stuck on top - works a treat!!

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: I wonder if the 365nm UV LEDs (that are supposedly the same frequency as the one you would care about in fluorescent black lights) would work faster, or damage the resin prints, or not work at all, period.

author: @C-M-E
text: While I wouldn't down a free nuker for the parts, I went stupid simple for my curing station: 5 gallon bucket, aluminized reflective tape, UVLED strip lights, $13 all in. Run the tape around the inside, adhere strip lights, and it's also nice to have a rack system for curing multiple prints vertically. I tend to print 7" figures I sculpt but I haven't come across anything that doesn't fit including some very large impeller models.

author: @mfahudson
text: Hi Merry christmas . Can you tell me why you out the UV lights at the back of the oven and not on the top . I been told not to look straight at  UV lights as it can damage your eyes best regards

replies.author: @Nick_Tag
replies.text: as others have mentioned the only UV bands you need to be aware of are just UV-a,b,c - this LED strip wouldn't damage dna etc the wavelength for that is 247nm if i'm not mistaken.

author: @Alacritous
text: Those UV LEDs won't kill bacteria. You need UVC for that.  And you'd have to cover the window or you'd damage your eyesight. Other than that, sure, it would work as a sterilizer.

author: @karlhill6652
text:
Love this, re purpose discarded stuff is a win for all. 
Just a thought, one could have used the original timer for auto functionality.
 Punch in the time wanted and (as you say) Hey presto! it swirls on for the time set and you don't need to worry about a separate timer while tinkering with other stuff. Also useful when making photo transfers to PCB's, erasing a bunch of EPROM's or sanitize all sorts of things from viruses, bacteria etc..   Merry Christmas Rob & Luke!🎄🎀🎁

author: @Kezat
text: Most microwaves I have taken to bits have a simple relay to control the transformer, so you can have a timer already built in using the existing control interface. I find It's so easy to forget and overcook parts in the UV light so having a timer is nice.

replies.author: @Kezat
replies.text: James Cunliffe Yup should work as well or who knows you might find "defrost vegetables"  to be a perfect time to cure your parts.

replies.author: @Kezat
replies.text: James Cunliffe You lost me, are you thinking of heating resin parts with microwaves? Does not sound like a good idea but if you do try it let us know how it goes :)

author: @Dr-Sy
text: I have a question,   If i take a ring magnet and and wrap copper wire around the magnet then place a live wire through the ring magnet, Will it produce a current from the field off the live wire?

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: You don't need a ring magnet, just an iron core ring used for inductors and smaller transformers. An inductor only has a single wrapping of insulated copper wire where a transformer has to or more, a primary and one or more secondaries.

replies.author: @Dr-Sy
replies.text: @stevetobias4890 could this be put around the ground wire of a home to to get a current?
